coredata.dc:description: The development of new materials and new technologies is indispensable to the research of new drug delivery systems. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP), synthesized via molecular imprinting technique (MIT) have attracted much attention in pharmaceutical fields due to its distinct advantages. In recent years, it has been found that these MIP-based drug delivery systems (MIP-DDS) can improve the therapeutic efficacy of the drugs as a new strategy for delivering therapeutic agents with desired controlled release and/or targeting efficiency. The anticipated in vivo performance for the MIP-DDS can be achieved through optimizing the components or by surface modification. What’s more, the MIP-DDS are also easy to prepare with low production costs, good biodegradability and biocompatibility, which is very beneficial for the applications of MIP in drug delivery systems. In the paper, we aim to summarize the present state and of MIP, including the structure, components, preparation method, especially its application in drug delivery, and pointed out the prospects and challenges. The study will provide a certain reference and the direction for the follow-up study on MIP in drug delivery.

head.ce:title: Advances of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) and the application in drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences, School of Basic Medical Sciences, Henan University of Science and Technology, Luoyang 471023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of new materials and new technologies is indispensable to the research of new drug delivery systems. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP), synthesized via molecular imprinting technique (MIT) have attracted much attention in pharmaceutical fields due to its distinct advantages. In recent years, it has been found that these MIP-based drug delivery systems (MIP-DDS) can improve the therapeutic efficacy of the drugs as a new strategy for delivering therapeutic agents with desired controlled release and/or targeting efficiency. The anticipated in vivo performance for the MIP-DDS can be achieved through optimizing the components or by surface modification. What’s more, the MIP-DDS are also easy to prepare with low production costs, good biodegradability and biocompatibility, which is very beneficial for the applications of MIP in drug delivery systems. In the paper, we aim to summarize the present state and of MIP, including the structure, components, preparation method, especially its application in drug delivery, and pointed out the prospects and challenges. The study will provide a certain reference and the direction for the follow-up study on MIP in drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: For many years, researchers have committed to developing novel drug delivery systems, such as targeted delivery system, controlled release system and so on. And certain achievements have been made, for example, Doxorubicin Hydrochloride Liposome Injection (Doxil®) and Glipizide extended release Tablets (GLUCOTROL XL®), which are the typical products for targeted delivery system and controlled release system, respectively. Novel delivery systems with certain advantages mainly rely on not only functional carriers or materials, but also the application of new technologies. Generally, modified or unmodified nanocarriers prepared by nanotechnology have the function of targeted delivery, reducing the non-specific adsorption and may prolong the half-life time of the drugs in serum, avoid the uptake by reticulo-endothelial system (RES) [1,2], which are extensively studied in tumor targeted delivery system. The controlled-release system needs the components with erosion, enteric solubility or hydrophobicity and so on [3,4]. For example, the formulations prepared by Osmotic pump technology can achieve the effect of controlled release with the release behavior of zero-order model [5]. Numerous researches about the drug delivery systems have been done focusing on increasing the therapeutic efficacy of the drugs with low side effect. However, only few of them are approved for clinical use. Research has never stopped on feasible drug delivery systems.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP), also known as “artificial antibodies”, have attracted the attention of researchers in the field of pharmacy due to their distinct advantages. MIP are the synthetic materials with certain space structure and strength prepared via molecularly imprinted technique. Its basic principle is displayed in Fig. 1 and described briefly as follow: the template molecule and functional monomer combine by covalent bond or non-covalent bond, and then polymerize in the presence of cross-linking agent, and lastly the template molecule is removed forming specific binding sites or empty cavities complementary to template molecules in size, shape and ligand [6,7].

Therefore, when MIP are used as the carriers, sustained drug release can be achieved due to the specific selectivity and affinity of MIP to template molecules, which make MIP to be a promising drug delivery system. And an intelligent MIP-DDS can be obtained by employing functional materials, including magnetic, pH sensitive, temperature sensitive, photo sensitive and bio-macromolecule sensitive materials [8–12]. The combination of molecular imprinting and stimulus response can not only improve the loading capacity of specific drug molecules, but also “intelligently” adjust the drug adsorption / release process according to the change of external environment. Additionally, the in vivo performance of MIP-DDS can be optimized by selecting its components and preparation methods according to the requirements. For example, the desired targeting effect can be achieved by surface modification with corresponding ligands [13]. More importantly, by optimizing the components, we can obtain the MIP with good biodegradability and biocompatibility, which is very beneficial for the applications of MIP in drug delivery systems [14].

Based on the developments and advantages of MIP, A lot of research has been done on MIP. And until now, several drug delivery systems that exploit MIP as carriers have been successfully fabricated, such as oral drug delivery systems for controlled release, intravenous drug delivery systems for targeted delivery. Therefore, we aimed to summarize the present state-of-the art of MIP-based drug delivery systems. The structure and composition, preparation methods, characterization and the potential applications are introduced in this review, and the current challenge and perspective of MIP are also discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and components of MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the three-dimensional (3D) network structure is the typical feature of MIP. And template, functional monomer, cross-linker, and initiator are necessary constituents to prepare MIP.

The templates are the substances that researchers are interested in or aim to separate, detect, or loaded for delivery, including small molecule organic matters and bio-macromolecules [8,14]. All the templates must possess enough activity to combine with the functional monomer to form a prepolymerization complex. Functional monomers, cross-linkers and initiators commonly used are shown in Table 1 . In an ideal polymers, functional monomer and cross-linker are very important, because they compose the skeleton of the polymer. A certain degree of hydrophilicity is needed for

them to increase the biocompatibility. Forming the stable prepolymerization complex is the key for the efficient molecular imprinting. The high stability of interaction between the template and monomer is conducive to the formation of uniform binding sites and minimizes the presence of non-specific sites. Therefore, it is important to select suitable functional monomers. Meanwhile, cross-linkers are used to form a three-dimensional network with moderate rigidity and flexibility. On the one hand, the existence of cross-linkers provides the rigidity of material and the space stability of cavity. On the other hand, the obtained polymer must be flexible enough to ensure that the appropriate solvent could diffuse easily into the matrix. Both highly crosslinked rigid MIP and low crosslinked imprinted hydrogels can be used for drug delivery.

Sometimes, a little porogen, for example dimethylsulfoxide, is used to modulate the property of the polymers, and also plays the role of solvent. The porogens are helpful for modifying the drug release from the MIP [19]. The functional materials, such as magnetic agents, photo/thermo/pH-sensitive constituents also are brought in the MIP to achieve the specific effect [13,19].

Bulk polymerization is a typical method for preparation of MIP. For example, Kobayashi prepared cyclodextrin-based porous polymeric membrane by bulk polymerization method [25]. During the preparation process, only the monomers, mononers and cross-linkers polymerize to form polymers without any solvent or other dispersants. And the polymerization need to be triggered by chemical initiator, light, heat, or radiation [26]. The method is simple and avoid other solvent used in the polymerization process. However, the polymers obtained via the method are lumpy and irregular. And in the follow-up application, the polymers need to be further ground and sieved, which could destroy the structure of polymers and the imprinting sites. And more importantly, the imprinting sites are deeply embedded by the highly cross-linked polymers. It is disadvantageous to elution and recombination, resulting in slow mass transfer rate, long equilibrium time and less binding [27].

Precipitation polymerization is known as “homogeneous solution polymerization”. Compared with the bulk polymerization, more solvent is used, which is the main feature of precipitation polymerization method [28]. In this preparation process, the functional monomer(s), template(s), cross-linker(s) and initiator(s) dissolved in copious amounts of solvent. The concentration of the system is low, and the polymer can grow independently. Once formed, the polymer will precipitate out of the solution. The obtained precipitation is loose and uniform without grinding after polymerization [29,30]. So, the binding site avoid to be damaged, and the imprinting effect is better than that by bulk polymerization. Based on the advantages, extensive researches reported that MIP were successfully prepared via precipitation polymerization method and applied for drug delivery through different routes, such as oral, intravenous, transdermal and ocular, achieving satisfied results [31–33].

Suspension polymerization is a commonly used method for preparation of MIP. The method is similar to the precipitation polymerization method except for the added stabilizer in the solvent. Because of the effect of stabilizer, the obtained polymers are suspended rather than precipitate in the system [34]. Usually, the method is mainly used to prepare molecularly imprinted polymeric microspheres with better size distribution and regular sphere. The size of the MIP microspheres could be controlled by modulating the parameters, such as stirring speed, the amount of cross-linker and so on. If the porous microspheres need to be prepared, a certain amount of porogen can be added into the system [35,36]. This method possess the advantages of simple synthesis step and convenient treatment of the product. Therefore, many researches based on MIP microspheres formulated by this method have been conducted [37,38].

Surface imprinting polymerization method is an attractive method for the preparation of MIP. The preparation process can be illustrated as displayed in Fig. 2 . Generally, proper nano-materials including SiO2 [39], metal-organic framework (MOF) materials [17,40], carbon nanotubes [41], carbon dots [42], are used as matrix carriers, and functional monomers are introduced on the surface of the matrix carriers by appropriate means such as grafting or chelation. Then the template combine to the monomer, forming prepolymerization complex, which is then cross-linked on the surface of the matrix carrier to form surface imprinting polymer, under the action of cross-linker and initiator. By the method, most of the template molecules exist on the polymer surface. That is to say that most of the imprinting sites of the MIP are on the surface, which is beneficial to the adsorption-desorption process of template molecules.

Additionally, surface imprinting polymerization method usually is used to prepare nano molecularly imprinted polymers (nanoMIP) with desired particle sizes and size distribution to being applied in targeted diagnosis and drug delivery [42,43]. It is reported that Dong, et al. [44] successfully prepared HER2-glycan-imprinted nanoparticles, SiO2 as the matrix carrier, which exhibited good dispersibility, homogeneous size distribution and superior antitumor efficiency in vitro and in vivo. Due to the advantages of the MIP prepared via the method, such as smaller sizes, better reproducibility and biocompatibility, surface imprinting polymerization method has been widely used to prepare MIP in recent years.

Based on surface imprinting polymerization method, the Boron affinity controllable-oriented surface molecular imprinting technology was developed, which effectively combines the Boron affinity with the molecular imprinting technology [45]. The imprinting process mainly contain three steps: template fixation, oriented imprinting and template removal [46]. Template fixation refers to the binding of template molecules to the substrate surface by Boron affinity (the formation of borate esters between the cis-dihydroxy group of template molecules and the boric acid functional groups on the substrate surface). Oriented imprinting is to polymerize a imprinting layer on the substrate surface and to customize the thickness of the imprinting layer according to the size of the template molecule. Template removal involves eluting the template molecules via appropriate means to form an imprinting cavity on the imprinting layer corresponding to the three-dimensional dimensions of the template molecules. This technology with the advantges of high imprinting efficiency, controllability, good versatility, high affinity, strong anti-interference ability and so on, was widely applied in enzyme activity analysis and disease diagnosis [47].

Some other methods are also used for preparation of MIP, such as emulsion polymerization, swelling polymerization and in-situ polymerization. Each method has its own characteristics and application fields.

In emulsion polymerization method, the suitable emulsifier is necessary to guarantee fine emulsion droplets. And then the initiator is added for cross-linking polymerization to obtain a spherical polymer with uniform particle size [14]. Swelling polymerization method is usually used to prepare MIP microspheres. For the swelling polymerization method, the seeds of microspheres are readied in advance, and then the template molecule, functional monomer, cross-linking agent, initiator and so on are put together with the seeds to get the product by swelling polymerization [48]. Although the preparation process is complex, the imprinted microspheres have uniform particle size and most of the binding sites are located on the surface of the polymer, which is conducive to the better adsorption kinetic properties. Generally, the preparation of a MIP chromatographic column or capillary adopt in-situ polymerization method, which involves injecting a prepolymerized mixture solution into an empty column or capillary, plugging the ends with a plug, and heating in a water bath to initiate polymerization [49]. Then the column or capillary is connected to a chromatograph or electrophoresis instrument to wash out the template. This method can complete the preparation of MIP and column loading in one step. Therefore the experimental process is greatly simplified.

For the methods, not only one template can be imprinted, but also two or more templates can be imprinted simultaneously to meet the needs of the application [43]. And during the preparation process, some parameters, such as the ratio of template molecule to monomer, the amount of crosslinking agent, temperature, the solvent and so on, need to be optimized in order to obtain the desired MIP.

Usually, the surface morphology, crystalline form, thermal stability, surface area, the pore parameters (size, specific volume), the number of binding sites (Nt), imprinting factor (IF), drug loading efficiency, size and size distribution, and the degree of swelling (Sw) are mainly evaluated to characterize MIP [17,50]. These characteristics are very important for the MIP because these factors not only effect drug loading efficiency and stability during the formulation process and storage, but also are related to their performance in vitro and in vivo [51,52]. What’s more, the drug loading efficiency and stability of MIP after drug loading also are studied. With the fast development of the detection technique, more and more methods are used to characterize MIP. Some of the common technologies used for characterizing MIP are summarized in Table 2 .

Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) can be used to identify the molecular structure of an unknown substance, and the strength of the absorption peak can be used to quantify the composition of the molecule or the content of the chemical group. In the characterization of MIP, comparatively analysing the infrared spectra of the template, monomer and anticipated MIP, specially some characteristic absorption peaks, whether the MIP is successfully prepared can be confirmed [51].

The MIP with uniform morphology are expected. To better observe the morphology of the MIP, SEM、TEM、AFM and STM are used. Additionally, in order to guarantee the stability and superior performance in vivo distribution for MIP microspheres and nanocarriers, the proper size and narrow size distribution are required. Certainly, the size and size distribution also can be detected by DLS [52]. Meanwhile, the imprinting cavities or holes also can display on the images of SEM、TEM、AFM and/or STM. The surface area and pore parameters can reflect thedegree of crosslinking and the number of the binding sites. As the number of binding sites increase, IF and drug loading increase. Generally, in the process of MIP preparation, IF is used as an evaluation index to optimize the preparation parameters [53]. The drug loading efficiency represents the capability of the MIP to load the therapeutic agents. And the definition of the drug loading efficiency is shown in Table 2. The degree of swelling correlated with the release behaviour of drug in the system is used to evaluate the MIP hydrogel [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of MIP in drug delivery systems
ce:section.ce:para: The MIP are first emerged in the field of enantiomeric resolution and now attract extensive attention in delivery systems [55]. MIP has some advantages such as biocompatible, low toxicity, biodegradability. Furthermore, its low cost, ease of preparation and multiple routes of administration make it an alternative carrier. Therefore, the MIP are developed as a good carrier in delivering drugs for treatments of various diseases such as cancer [56], arrhythmia[57,58], avitaminosis [59], cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases [16], inflammation [32], addictive disease [33,60] and so on. And they can be administrated via different ways, such as oral, intravenous, and so on (Table 3 ). Here we summarize mainly four delivery systems which use MIP as the carrier achieving superior effect.

Oral administration is the most common and convenient route of administration. Due to the low oral bioavailability and side effects, oral administration in clinic is restricted for most of the drugs with poor solubility. The development of satisfactory oral drug delivery forms remains a major challenge. MIP play important role in the researches. On the one hand, the affinity between MIP and the imprinted drug molecules lead to a sustained release [16,59]. On the other hand, because the affinity can be effected by some factors, such as pH, temperature and competitive conjugates, the three-dimensional network structure of MIP and the binding of drugs to MIP will change under the corresponding conditions [62]. Therefore, smart release systems could be designed.

5-fluorouracil (5-FU) is a chemotherapy drug with a wide spectrum of anti-cancer and high efficiency, especially the preferred chemotherapy drug for the treatment of colorectal cancer [63,64]. But some disadvantages exist, such as fast metabolism (half-life, t1/2 10–20 min), serious gastrointestinal reactions, bone marrow cytotoxicity, and poor selectivity for tumour cells [65,66].

Numerous studies were carried out aiming to develop new formulations to improve the chemotherapy efficiency and avoid side effects [48,67]. It is reported that, a new oral colon-specific 5-FU delivery system with CS-PMMA as matrix microspheres and 5-FU as template molecule was developed via molecular surface imprinted technique. In the study, the prepared MIP exhibited high recognition selectivity and excellent combining affinity to 5-FU. And the in vitro release of 5-FU was highly pH-dependent and time delayed: no release in simulated gastric fluid (pH = 1.0), a little release in the simulated small intestinal fluid (pH = 6.8), and abrupt release in the simulated colon fluid (pH = 7.4). The results indicated excellent colon-specific drug delivery behaviour, which meant the oral colon-specific 5-FU delivery system will be hoped to apply for treatment of colon and colorectal cancer with high efficacy and safety [68].

Zhang, et al firstly fabricated the multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) coated liquid crystalline-molecularly imprinted polymer (LC-MIP) as a novel floating interaction-controlled DDS [61] (Fig. 3 ). MWCNTs@LC-MIP was successfully prepared with co-polymerization of methacrylic acid, 4-methyl phenyl dicyclohexyl ethylene, and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate, levofloxacin (LVF) as the template drug via surface molecularly imprinting polymerization method. And the in vitro study suggested that the LVF had a satisfied controlled release behavior from MWCNTs@LC-MIP, and the relative bioavailability of LVF in MWCNTs@LC-MIP was significantly increased (578.9%). The MWCNTs@LC-MIP showed potentials as a carrier for oral administration by the innovative combination of floating and controlled release properties.

In conclusion, all these results shown that MIP might be developed and used as an oral controlled release delivery systems for therapeutic agents to improve their therapy efficiency and avoid side effects and toxicities.

It is well known that cancer is a serious threat to human health, but effective treatment is still a major challenge. Although there's a variety of treatments against tumours, chemotherapy is still one of the main treatment methods [69,70]. In order to increase the efficacy and reduce the side effects and toxicity of the chemotherapeutic drugs, the researchers have been working on targeted delivery systems for many years. The MIP have attracted extensive attention and been deeply studied not only for targeted diagnosis but also targeted drug delivery [71]. MIP was investigated for delivery of chemotherapeutic drugs showing excellent targeting ability and desirable treatment effect, what’s more, the toxicity to healthy tissues and organs was negligible.

Jia et al investigated the way for formulation of imprinted polymer nanoparticle (MIPNPs), bleomycin (BLM) and 71–80 peptide of human fibroblast growth-factor-inducible 14 modified with glucose (Glu-FH) as the dual-templates [43]. The prepared MIPNPs could load BLM and Glu-FH simultaneously forming FH-MIPNPs/BLM. The in vitro results shown that FH-MIPNPs could precisely target to BxPC-3 cells, which overexpress human fibroblast growth-factor-inducible 14 (FN14). While the targeting effect could not be detected on cells without expression of FN14. The in vivo antitumor experiments shown that the tumour volume of xenografts tumour of BxPC-3 increased to 1.05×, 1.5×, 1.6×, and 2.4×, respectively for the different groups treated with FH-MIPNPs/BLM, BLM, FH-NIPNPs/BLM, and physiological saline (Fig. 4 ). Above results shown that FH-MIPNPs/BLM had obvious targeted therapeutic effect.

Numerous researches proved that blocking the HER2 signal pathway using monoclonal antibodies and tyrosine-kinase inhibitors has been an effective strategy against HER2-positive breast cancer [72]. However effective blocking is the main challenge. Aiming to inhibiting HER2 + breast cancer growth via binding almost all HER2 glycans and suppress the dimerization of HER2 with other HER family members to blocking the downstream signal pathways, Dong et al present a new strategy, the nano molecularly imprinted polymer based delivery system (nanoMIP-DDS), in which HER2 N-glycans was the template [44]. And then in vitro experiments were conducted and proved that nanoMIP-DDS specifically targeted HER2 + cells and inhibited cell proliferation by 30%. Further in vivo study indicated that the mean tumour volume in the nanoMIP-DDS treated group was only about half of that in the controlled group. NanoMIP-DDS provided a new possibility to treat of HER2 + breast cancer.

All results suggested that imprinted polymers are becoming to a promising platform for integrated tumour-targeted diagnosis and treatment. What’s more, all the studies boost further development of nanoMIP-DDS for cancer therapy.

Traditional ophthalmic preparations (e.g. eye drops) are poorly efficient for ocular drug delivery. Poor bioavailability and uncontrollable drug delivery are the main challenges in ocular drug delivery [73]. Recently, molecularly imprinted soft contact lenses have been reported as novel ocular drug delivery systems with several advantages: increasing of loading capacity, optimization of drug residence time and biocompatibility with the eye tissue [74–77].

Malakooti, et al prepared a molecularly imprinted polymer 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) hydrogel to increase drug loading and sustain the drug release [75]. In the study, HEMA-drug-functional monomer-cross-linker molar ratios were optimized to prepare polymyxin B imprinted and non-imprinted hydrogel. The results shown that acrylic acid-functionalized and imprinted hydrogel loaded more polymyxin B and led to sustained release profiles compared with non-functionalized and non-imprinted networks. And microbiological assays carried out with Pseudomonas aeruginosa proved the efficient bacteria eradication for the most suitable formulation. Silva, D designed imprinted silicon based hydrogels to load the antibiotic drug moxifloxacin hydrochloride (MXF) and evaluated the feasibility as therapeutic soft contact lenses (SCLs) [75]. In the imprinted silicon based hydrogel, acrylic acid was as the functional monomer which may establish potential interactions with MXF. After extensive washing, MXF was loaded by soaking the hydrogel in MFX solution (2.5 mg/mL) at room temperature for 72 h. The following transmittance, swelling and oxygen permeability evaluation suggested that the hydrogel was suitable for daily contact lenses wearing. Meanwhile, the hydrogel had a sustained release effect. And the MXF concentration profile in the tear fluid estimated from the mathematical model indicated that the release of MXF from the imprinted hydrogel maintain 48 h above the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) for different microorganisms typically colonized in the eyes, while drug released from the non-imprinted hydrogel was about 10 h. In summary, the study demonstrated that the prepared imprinted silicone hydrogel was able to host large amount of MXF and prolong drug release. These imprinted materials could be used for the design of daily therapeutic SCLs.

The studies reported all illustrate that MIP-DDS is an alternative ocular drug delivery system, which could be used as therapeutic soft contact lenses. Compared to other ocular drug delivery systems, the novel MIP ocular drug delivery system possess the advantages of enhancing drug-loading capacity and controlling the drug release.

Except the applications of MIP in oral and intravenous delivery system, the most common routes of administration, the application of MIP in transdermal drug delivery system also attracts researchers’ attention [78,79]. Ruela, et al prepared MIP by a free radical polymerisation method with nicotine as the template to evaluate the feasibility of MIP used for the controlled transdermal delivery of nicotine [33,60]. After characterization, such as FTIR, thermal analysis and SEM, polymer particles were included in transdermal systems with vehicles of different polarities. In the studies, authors investigated the effect of preparation method, monomer, cross-linker on the drug release behaviour. What’s more, skin permeation studies were also carried out using ear porcine skin. The results suggested that MIP based formulations could control the skin permeation flux fitting to zero order kinetic for prolonged times (48 h), and the permeation rate from these MIP formulations was lower than that from NIP formulations. The authors also pointed out that the skin permeation behaviour of nicotine was associated with limited swelling of MIP. That is to say, we could select apposite preparation method and optimize the formulation to achieve the desired controlled release effect. MIP-DDS can be considered as a suitable candidate for the controlled transdermal delivery system.

For an excellent drug delivery systems or carriers, in addition to higher drug-loading capacity and controlled drug release, biocompatibility and biodegradability are necessary. Fortunately, MIP possess the superiority due to the main ingredients (functional monomer and cross-linker) with biocompatibility and biodegradability. For example, Gelatin, an amphiphilic substance, not only contains abundant active groups (–NH2, –COOH, and –OH) but also is a nontoxic, nonimmunogenic, and biodegradable macromolecule. Therefore, gelatin is an ideal functional monomer to develop MIP with good biocompatibility [80].

MIP emerges as a promising drug carrier mainly in the fields of sustained release, controlled release and targeted delivery system based on its distinct advantages. While it is still in its infancy, more extensive and in-depth research is yet to be undertaken to develop a safe and effective MIP-DDS. Firstly, stability, biocompatibility and toxicity, especially long term toxicity, need to be further studied, especially when MIP is applied in intravenous drug delivery systems. Secondly, most of MIP are synthesized in organic solvent, in which organic solvent residue need to be strictly controlled. And the synthesis of MIP for water soluble drug and biological macromolecules such as polypeptides were deficient. Thirdly, more ideal ingredients for preparing molecularly imprinted polymers, such as functional monomers, need to be developed to meet the clinical applications. Computer-based high-throughput screening techniques can be requisitioned. Lastly, transformation from bench to clinical application and large-scale production are the key problems to be solved for the successfully application of MIP in clinic. Although MIP still has the above mentioned shortcomings, its controlled release and targeting are still significant advantages in drug delivery, especially, it has a good application prospect in the targeted diagnosis and treatment of tumors. In a word, with the progress of molecular imprinting technology and the in-depth study of MIP in drug delivery systems, MIP-DDS will play an important role in the treatment of diseases as a promising drug delivery system.

Suna He: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Liping Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Shangkun Bai: Writing - review & editing. Hui Yang: Validation. Zheng Cui: Writing - review & editing. Xiaofei Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Yuanpei Li: Writing - review & editing.
